narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 91.9% chance that you would not pass, based on patterns from thousands of past students' academic and background details. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection to help the university maintain strong bar passage rates for its graduates. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your academic decile rankings from school, particularly your Year 3 performance, which stood out as the top factor pushing toward this failure prediction—your placement in the lowest decile there signaled a notable gap compared to the much higher averages (around the 6th decile) among students who went on to pass the bar. Your Year 1 decile, also in the bottom rung versus the typical 6th decile for passers, ranked as the second-biggest influence, further reinforcing the model's concern about early struggles that often persist. While your LSAT score of 37 was close to the average for bar passers at 37.27 and actually nudged the prediction slightly in a positive direction, it wasn't enough to offset those academic rankings. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, just below the 3.24 average for successful students, and your upper-middle family income bracket—which aligns well with 45% of bar passers—played supporting roles but weren't the main drivers. Being a full-time student is a common strength, matching 93% of those who passed, yet it couldn't counterbalance the key academic flags. Overall, your profile compares to accepted applicants by lagging significantly in those early and later school deciles, where top performers typically cluster in the upper half. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven flag on bar success odds. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through targeted tutoring or additional undergrad-level work to lift your GPA, and reapply once you've strengthened those areas—many students improve dramatically with focused effort. You might also explore bar prep resources early or schools with more flexible admission criteria. Keep pushing; your LSAT shows promise, and refining these other elements could shift the odds in your favor next time.
